text: The surgeon begins lateral pelvic lymph node dissection by identifying and retracting the ureter medially, preserving the ureterohypogastric fascia to protect the hypogastric nerve plexus. Attention shifts to the external iliac vessels, where lymphofatty tissues are dissected from the external iliac artery and vein. Dissection proceeds laterally, revealing the obturator internus muscles, marking the lateral margin. The obturator nerve is exposed between the bifurcation of the external and internal iliac veins, preserved unless involved by metastatic nodes. The obturator artery, bifurcating from the internal iliac artery, is dissected and ligated, along with the proximal and distal ends of the obturator vein. Lymphofatty tissue along the obturator nerve is dissected to the obturator foramen, and the distal end of the obturator artery is ligated, ensuring complete lymphatic clearance in the obturator space. Dissection continues distally, with the deep circumflex iliac vein marking the caudal limit anteriorly.

The surgeon performs dissection along the vesico-hypogastric plane, exposing the internal iliac artery and its branches. The umbilical artery is dissected and ligated at its root, while the superior vesicle artery is preserved. Lymphatic tissue between the internal iliac vein and ureterohypogastric fascia is dissected. The inferior vesicle artery is dissected and ligated at its root. Dissection continues between the bladder and lymphatic tissue, with distal vesicle artery and veins ligated at the bladder side using advanced energy devices or hemlock clips.

The surgeon dissects the most caudal part of the lymphatic tissue beyond the vesicle vessels, within a space bounded by the lateral pelvic sidewall, levator ani muscles, and medial pelvic plexuses. The resected specimen is placed in a bag and removed, achieving complete clearance of the lateral pelvic lymph nodes while preserving the hypogastric nerve plexus.
